title: The polymerase inhibition assay: A methodology for the identification of DNA-damaging agents
abstract:
The Polymerase Inhibition Assay:
A Methodology for the Identi®cation of
DNA-Damaging Agents
Gareth J. S. Jenkins,1* Brian Burlinson,2 and James M. Parry1
1University of Wales Swansea, Swansea, Wales, United Kingdom
2Glaxo Wellcome Pharmaceuticals, Ware, Herts, United Kingdom
We report here the development of the polymerase inhibition assay (PI assay), a methodology capable of
simultaneously identifying multiple DNA-damaging agents. The PI assay was developed in order to fulfil a requirement
for the screening of new pharmaceuticals for potential DNA-damaging effects. The assay has the potential to screen
hundreds of new compounds per week because of the microtiter plate format employed. We review previous
descriptions of the phenomenon and provide researchers with the necessary methodology to obtain optimum
polymerase inhibition effects. The assay is based on the inhibition of DNA polymerases (including those used in the
polymerase chain reaction (PCR)) encountering damaged DNA bases. Hence, DNA-damaging agents can be identified
by a corresponding reduction in PCR amplification after exposure. We demonstrate the detection of polymerase
inhibition induced by a range of model genotoxic agents (N-methyl-N-nitrosourea, N-ethyl-N-nitrosourea, and
ultraviolet (UV) C radiation), illustrating the successful application of the methodology. In addition, the PI assay is
shown to be capable of detecting DNA damaging agents of biological relevance, i.e., known human carcinogens.
These were N-OH-PhIP (from cooked meat) and UV-B (from sunlight). In addition to its employment in the detection
of putative DNA damaging agents, the PI assay may also be applied as a research tool in carcinogenicity studies.
Mol. Carcinog. 27:289±297, 2000.
ß 2000 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
Key words: DNA damage; polymerase inhibition; PCR; carcinogenesis
INTRODUCTION
The occurrence of DNA damage is known to be
an initial step in mutation induction whereby
damaged bases are misreplicated during DNA synth-
esis by DNA polymerase [1]. Mutagenesis is itself a
known precursor of carcinogenesis, when mutations
are induced in key genes that are involved in the
control of cell division [2]. Therefore, the identi®ca-
tion
of
DNA-damaging
agents
(genotoxins)
is
essential if a reduction in human exposures to
potential carcinogens is to be achieved. In addition,
the accumulation of information on the process of
DNA damage formation may lead to a better under-
standing of mutagenesis and hence cancer induc-
tion.
Sensitive
methodologies
to
detect
DNA
damage (genotoxicity) may provide us with the
ability to identify new DNA-reactive agents. Com-
pounds identi®ed as potent DNA-damaging agents
may have the potential to be developed as anti-
tumor agents.
The identi®cation of DNA-damaging agents is
especially important for the pharmaceutical indus-
try, in which new pharmaceuticals must be screened
for genotoxicity before their widespread distribu-
tion. With the recent advent of combinatorial
chemistry [3,4] and the subsequent increase in the
number of new compounds available, the demand
placed upon current genotoxicity tests has increased
by several orders of magnitude. This has led to the
requirement for new methodologies to cope with
this scale of screening. It is envisaged that the
polymerase inhibition assay (PI assay) would be
employed as part of a battery of high-throughput
genotoxicity tests to identify DNA-damaging agents.
Methodological developments in the identi®cation
of genotoxins may also bene®t a wide range of
researchers in the associated ®eld of carcinogenesis.
This paper describes the development of the PI
assay as a methodology to identify DNA-damaging
agents, with the potential for application in high-
throughput screening. The PI assay exploits the
well-reported fact that DNA damage blocks DNA
synthesis both in vitro and in vivo [5,6]. This DNA
damage can take the form of DNA adducts or abasic
sites and/or strand breaks.
In practice, templates containing DNA damage
are known to be poor substrates for the polymerase
chain reaction (PCR) [7,8], whereas nonadducted
templates can be readily ampli®ed. Hence, the
MOLECULAR CARCINOGENESIS 27:289±297 (2000)
ß 2000 WILEY-LISS, INC.
*Correspondence to: University of Wales Swansea, Singleton
Park, Swansea, SA2 8PP.
Received 8 October 1999; Revised 16 November 1999; Accepted
14 December 1999
Abbreviations: PI assay, polymerase inhibition assay; PCR, poly-
merase chain reaction; B&W, binding and washing; UV, ultraviolet;
ENN, N-ethyl-N-nitrosourea; MNU, N-methyl-N-nitrosourea; N-OH-
PHIP, N-hydroxy-2-amino-1-methyl-6-phenylimidazo[4,5-6]pyridine;
DMSO, dimethylsulfoxide.
 presence of DNA damage in target DNA sequences
can be deduced from the accompanying reduction
in PCR ampli®cation. The use of PCR allows us to
harness the exponential ampli®cation of this tech-
nique to distinguish between undamaged and
damaged DNA templates [9]. The simplicity of the
PI assay makes it amenable to medium- to high-
throughput screening requiring very little DNA (and
very little test compound) and no radiolabeling. We
report the developmental steps taken to optimize
the methodology by using model physical and
chemical DNA damaging agents. A wide range of
genotoxins have been shown to block DNA poly-
merases (Table 1) and hence should be detectable in
the PI assay. Figure 1 illustrates the methodology
employed in the PI assay using 96-well plates.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Preparation of the Target PCR Product
The PCR target was ampli®ed from mouse geno-
mic DNA by using primers designed for the p53
gene. Speci®cally, exons 4, 5, and 6 plus introns 4, 5,
and 6 were ampli®ed by the primers, giving a PCR
product of 1686 bp. PCR primers were obtained
from Cruachem Ltd (Glasgow, U.K.) and are shown

chain reaction (PCR) [7,8], whereas nonadducted
templates can be readily ampli®ed. Hence, the

nique to distinguish between undamaged and

report the developmental steps taken to optimize
the methodology by using model physical and
chemical DNA damaging agents. A wide range of
genotoxins have been shown to block DNA poly-
merases (Table 1) and hence should be detectable in
the PI assay. Figure 1 illustrates the methodology

Preparation of the Target PCR Product
The PCR target was ampli®ed from mouse geno-
mic DNA by using primers designed for the p53
gene. Speci®cally, exons 4, 5, and 6 plus introns 4, 5,
and 6 were ampli®ed by the primers, giving a PCR
product of 1686 bp. PCR primers were obtained
from Cruachem Ltd (Glasgow, U.K.) and are shown
Table 1. DNA Damaging Agents Shown to Inhibit DNA Polymerase
Figure 1. Schematic representation of the methodology involved in the PI assay.
in Table 2 (primers for other targets used in the
comparative target size study are also shown). PCR
ampli®cation was performed with 500 ng of mouse
genomic DNA (Promega Corporation, Southamp-
ton, U.K.) and 1 U of Extensor DNA polymerase
(Advanced Biotechnologies, Epson, U.K.) along with
50 mM dNTPs (Boehringer Mannheim-Roche Diag-
was performed by using a DNA engine PTC200 (MJ
Research, Watertown, MA) with hot-lid capability,
adapted to operate with 48-well microtiter plates.
Ampli®cation was performed with a touchdown
PCR program, in which denaturation was initially
performed at 94C for 2 min followed by three
cycles of 94C for 1 min, 68C for 1 min, and 72C
for 2 min 30 s. Following this, a further 18 cycles
were performed with a reduced annealing tempera-
The ampli®ed PCR products were assessed by 1%
agarose (Appligene-Oncor, Watford, U.K.) gel elec-
trophoresis with a DNA molecular weight marker
(Molecular Probes, Leiden, The Netherlands). The
concentration of the PCR product was assessed by
spectrophotometry at 260 nm with a DU530 spec-
trophotometer (Beckman, Fullerton, CA). The PCR
product was stored at ÿ20C as a stock solution prior
to dilution and exposure to DNA damaging agents.
Immobilization of the Target PCR Product
The target PCR product was thawed on ice and
diluted to 40 pg/mL in 10 mM Tris-HCl and 1 mM
EDTA, pH 7.5. This approximates to 107 copies/mL of
a 1700-bp DNA fragment. The thawing and dilution
steps were performed before each immobilization.
The PCR products were immobilized onto strepta-
primer in the initial PCR ampli®cation. The target
PCR product (80 pg) was added to each well of the
microtiter plate in a volume of 20 mL, along with an
equal volume of binding and washing (B&W) buffer
(10 mM Tris-HCl, pH 7.5, 1 mM EDTA, and 2 M
NaCl). A master mix of the PCR product and the
B&W buffer was produced to reduce any well-to-well
variation. Incubation was performed for 60 min at
room temperature, with regular mixing.
Immobilized target PCR products were exposed to
varying amounts of ultraviolet (UV) C (254 nm) and
UV-B (302 nm) light. The treatments were applied
dry, to prevent the interference of evaporation. The
UV-C radiation was administered from a Phillips T
UV lamp (30 W) at a dose rate of 6 J/m2/s. The UV-B
radiation was similarly administered from a Syn-
gene GLM-6 lamp (6 W) at a dose rate of 50 J/m2/s.
The dose rates were calibrated by using a UV
dosimeter (Unigalvo, Halstead, U.K.)
Immobilized PCR targets were treated in a Phar-
maceutical Safety Cabinet (Medical Air Technology,
U.K.) with a number of genotoxic chemicals. These
chemicals were dissolved in appropriate solvents,
and the dosing series were produced by dilution.
Control samples were treated with the solvent only.
The chemicals used were N-ethyl-N-nitrosourea
hydroxy-2-amino-1-methyl-6-phenylimidazo[4,5-b]
pyridine (N-OH-PhIP). Both ENU and MNU were
obtained from SigmaAldrich (Poole, U.K.) and were
dissolved in 20% dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) (Sig-
maAldrich) at top doses of 50 mM and 20 mM,
respectively. N-OH-PhIP was a gift from A. Williams,
(Institute of Cancer Research, Sutton, U.K.) and was
supplied in 100% DMSO. The N-OH-PhIP was
diluted in 10% DMSO to give a top dose of 450 mM.
Treatments with the chemical agents were per-
formed overnight at room temperature in the dark.
After treatment, the chemical was removed from
each well of the microtiter plate by using a multi-
immobilized products were then carefully washed
three times with 100 mL of B&W solution and left
dry prior to PCR reampli®cation.
The treated PCR targets were reampli®ed by using
the nested primers (Cruachem Ltd) shown in
Table 2, giving a nested product of 1542 bp. The
nested PCR consisted of 80 pg of immobilized target
Table 2. PCR Primers for the 1700-bp Target Used in This Study*
*B The primers for the comparative study (Figure 2) are shown.
PCR product, 1 U of Extensor DNA polymerase
(Advanced Biotechnologies), 50 mM dNTPs (Boeh-
Biotechnologies) in 1 PCR buffer (Advanced Bio-
technologies). The nested PCR was performed in
adapted DNA engine (MJ Research). The thermal
pro®le employed in the nested PCR consisted of a
denaturing step of 94C for 2 min, followed by
either 19 (UV treatments) or 23 cycles (chemical
treatments) of 94C for 30 s, 68C for 30 s, and 72C
Assessment of Polymerase Inhibition
The reampli®ed nested PCR products were elec-
trophoresed on 1% agarose gels and stained with
SybrGold (Molecular Probes, Leiden, The Nether-
lands). The band intensities were analyzed by using
the Quantity One software of the Gel Doc 2000
image analysis equipment (Bio-Rad, Hemel Hemp-
stead, U.K.). Relative PCR ampli®cations of the
damaged samples were calculated based on the
ampli®cation of the undamaged control, which
was assigned an ampli®cation factor of 100% by
AD  Relative ampli®cation of damaged sample
ID  Intensity of PCR band of damaged sample
I0  Intensity of PCR band of undamaged sample.
formed independently in triplicate, and the average
relative PCR intensities were calculated and plotted
on graphs of relative PCR against dose, including
standard errors. The relative ampli®cation was
calculated before calculating the standard errors,
in order to remove any gel-to-gel variation and
hence the untreated controls all had set values of
100% and do not contain standard errors.
Lesion frequencies were calculated from the dose
responses obtained by using the following equation
s  Lesion frequency of the strand (1542 bp)
AD  Ampli®cation of damaged DNA
A0  Ampli®cation of undamaged DNA.
The lesion frequency was calculated from the
minus value of the log of the relative PCR ampli®ca-
tion. This calculated lesion frequency refers to the
lesion frequency per 1542-bp strand; these values
were normalized to a value per 1 kb. The equation of
the best ®t line of lesion frequency against dose was
used to normalize the data in order to compare the
lesion frequency at a particular dose to previously
published data. It should be noted that the lesion
frequency calculated represents the average fre-
quency of polymerase-blocking lesions, which may
lesions, if such lesions do not inhibit PCR.
The PI assay was optimized by using UV-C light.
UV-C is the most reactive part of UV light and
represents a model DNA-damaging agent. With this,
all factors affecting polymerase inhibition were
optimized in turn. The ®ndings of these investiga-
tions are outlined here. First, the most critical factor
in polymerase inhibition is that the PCR ampli®ca-
tion of the damaged versus undamaged strands is
performed within the exponential phase of the PCR
[7,10]. Performing the experiments within the
exponential phase maximizes the relative ampli®ca-
tion differences between damaged and undamaged
strands, as the amount of ampli®able template is the
only rate-limiting step. Hence, the cycle numbers
employed here represent those falling in the mid-
exponential range of ampli®cation (results not
shown). In addition, the size of the target used in
the PI assay critically controls the sensitivity [8].
Any concern over the quantitative nature of the
assay can be overcome by including a 50% DNA
concentration control to ensure that the ampli®ca-
tion of this remains 50% of the control sample [10].
Figure 2 shows the effect of UV-C treatment on four
PCR targets of different sizes: the longer targets were
substantially more sensitive to UV-C radiation
Figure 2. Comparative polymerase inhibition. PCR targets of 700,
1000, 1700, and 3000 bp were exposed to UV-C radiation to
examine the effect of target length within the PI assay. Primers used
exposure, highlighting the proportional relation-
ship between target length and sensitivity. Other
factor found to in¯uence the sensitivity of the assay
were: the use of a proofreading Taq polymerase
instead of a standard Taq polymerase [11], the use of
low dNTP and MgCl2 concentrations [12,13] and the
use of limiting amounts of DNA in the treatment
and reampli®cation steps to reduce the chance of
undamaged strands remaining to seed the PCR
The Detection of UV Radiation±Induced DNA Damage
Although the sensitivity of the 3-kb target was
shown to be greater than that of the 1.7-kb target
in Figure 2, the 1.7-kb target was subsequently
employed, as its ampli®cation was more robust, i.e.,
less prone to day-to-day variation in ampli®cation
obtained with UV-C radiation in the PI assay. In
addition, Table 3 shows the lesion frequencies
obtained in our investigations and compares them
to the available published data. Figure 3 demon-
strates the ef®cient detection of UV-C±induced DNA
damage by the PI assay, in which doses as low as
25 J/m2 reduced ampli®cation substantially and
doses greater than 100 J/m2 appeared to reduce
PCR ampli®cation almost completely. The lesion
frequency can be extrapolated to produce a normal-
ized value of 1.22 lesions per kb per 100 J/m2. From
Table 3, it is evident that the lesion frequencies
detected here are approximately twofold to three-
fold higher than those detected in previous cell-
The PI assay was extended to analyze DNA
damage induced by the weaker part of UV light,
UV-B. UV-B is a biologically relevant genotoxin, as it
has been implicated in sunlight induced skin cancer
[24] Figure 4 shows the dose response obtained with
UV-B light, showing the decrease in ampli®cation
accompanying UV-B exposure. The normalized
lesion frequency calculated from Figure 4 was
0.0076 lesions per kb per 100 J/m2. The comparison
of UV-B and UV-C with regard to DNA damage
shows that UV-C was approximately 160-fold more
The Detection of Chemically Induced DNA Damage
The identi®cation of DNA-damaging chemical
agents required signi®cant modi®cations to the PI
assay, compared with the detection of UV radiation±
induced DNA damage. The necessary modi®cations
are set out in the Materials and Methods section. It
was found that the reampli®cation, after chemical
treatment, required four extra cycles of PCR (23
instead of 19 compared to UV treatments) in order
to generate adequate dose response curves. This
increase in cycle number was presumably due to the
loss of immobilized targets with the extra washing
steps required and perhaps due to the exposure to
the solvents used in the chemical dosing. However,
Figure 3. The Polymerase Inhibition by UV-C radiation using the
1700 bp target. DNA damage, suf®cient to reduce PCR ampli®ca-
tion, is induced by doses as low as 25 J/m2.
Table 3. Comparison of Lesion Frequency Data Obtained Here and Available in Previous
1.22 lesions per kb per 100 J/m2
0.4 lesions per kb per 100 J/m2
0.37 lesions per kb per 100 J/m2*
0.5 lesions per kb per 100 J/m2y
Chemical-induced lesion frequency
MNU 0.76 lesions per kb per 100 mM
ENU 0.011 lesions per kb per 100 mM
N-OH-PhIP 0.086 lesions per kb per 100 mM
*Extrapolated from a value of 0.37 lesions per 10 kb per 10 J/m2.
yExtrapolated from 1 lesion per 20 kb per 10 J/m2.
zExtrapolated from a value of 0.55 lesions per kb per 5 mM.
any artifactual effect of the solvent or the washing
was taken into account by the use of relative
ampli®cation to identically treated control samples.
Figures 5 and 6 show the dose responses of MNU
and ENU, respectively. Both show the reduced
ampli®cation accompanying dose-dependent DNA-
damage induction. MNU appear to be a more potent
DNA-damaging agent than ENU with regard to
polymerase inhibition. The lesion frequencies cal-
culated for MNU and ENU were 0.76 lesions per kb
per 100 mM and 0.011 lesions per kb per 100 mM,
respectively (ENU lesion frequency was extrapolated
from the value at 5 mM). The polymerase inhibition
induced by ENU was apparent only at doses greater
than 5 mM; at lower doses there appeared to be no
dose-dependent reduction in PCR ampli®cation.
The sensitivity of the PI assay in detecting the
alkylation of MNU compare well to data on the
lethal doses of this agent. We have been able to
show that MNU is lethal to human thyroid cells
when dosed at levels greater than 250 mM (data not
shown), whereas the PI assay detects MNU-induced
DNA damage at doses as low as 50 mM.
The sublethal doses of ENU employed in previous
1±2 mM range [25,26] and as such are slightly lower
than the doses shown to be effective here with the PI
assay (5 mM). The lesion frequencies of MNU and
ENU, shown in Table 3, allowed the calculation of
the comparative genotoxicity. MNU was demon-
strated to be approximately 70-fold more genotoxic
than ENU, with the PI assay. This supports pre-
viously published data on the genotoxicity of these
two alkylating agents showing that methylating
agents in general are 10- to 20-fold more DNA
reactive than ethylating agents [27].
Figure 7 shows the dose response of N-OH-PhIP
obtained with the PI assay, demonstrating the dose-
dependent decrease in PCR ampli®cation accompa-
nying DNA damage formation. N-OH-PhIP is a
Figure 4. UV-B induced polymerase inhibition, showing the
reduction in PCR ampli®cation at doses greater than 1000 J/m2.
Figure 5. MNU-induced polymerase inhibition, showing a reduc-
tion in PCR ampli®cation at doses greater than 50 mM.
Figure 6. ENU-induced polymerase inhibition, showing a reduc-
tion in PCR ampli®cation at doses greater than 5 mM.
Figure 7. N-OH-PhIP±induced polymerase inhibition, showing a
reduction in PCR ampli®cation at doses greater than 50 mM.
reactive metabolite of the cooked meat carcinogen
PhIP [28]. The polymerase inhibition of N-OH-PhIP
appeared to be effective only at doses greater than
50 mM, with lower doses having no apparent effect.
This is consistent with published data in which DNA
damage was shown to be induced by N-OH-PhIP in a
similar dose range (10±40 mM) [23]. The lesion
frequency calculated for N-OH-PhIP was found to
be 0.086 lesions per kb per 100 mM.
This report describes the development of the PI
assay as a screening system for pharmaceutical
compounds. The development of the PI assay
involved optimizing the steps in both the DNA-
damage induction and the subsequent PCR ampli-
®cation. The PI assay may now be amenable to
medium- to high-throughput screening of DNA-
damaging agents. The methodology can currently
be employed to screen approximately 100 com-
pounds per week (one 96-well plate format, ®ve
doses of each compound, one plate per day). This
may be increased by scaling up the PI assay to 384-
well plates. The PI assay may now partly ful®ll the
requirements of the pharmaceutical industry for
screening the large numbers of compounds now
becoming available. The PI assay is also amenable
for studying carcinogenesis by analyzing the effects
of DNA-damage induction and DNA repair.
The sensitivity of the assay was shown to be
capable of detecting DNA damage induced by both
model DNA-damaging agents (UV-C, MNU, and
ENU) and by weaker DNA-damaging agents, impli-
cated in human carcinogenesis (UV-B and N-OH-
PhIP). Therefore, the sensitivity of PI assay appears
to be within the range of biological relevance and
hence does not require extrapolation from very high
doses of genotoxins, as is necessary with some
techniques. The sensitivity achieved with the PI
assay relied upon several approaches taken in this
study. Initial studies using genomic DNA as a target
potential problems for the assay. First, genomic
DNA targets collected DNA damage mostly in
regions that were not subsequently ampli®ed, i.e.,
nontarget sites. Second, genomic DNA was dif®cult
to dose with chemical genotoxins, because of the
ethanol precipitations necessary to remove such
chemicals after-exposure. Therefore, we chose to
immobilize a PCR product as a target for DNA-
damage induction, overcoming both problems. The
improvement in sensitivity by employing a PCR
target compared with treating cells in vitro can be
frequency obtained in this report with that seen
previously when cells were treated with UV-C (Table
3). By employing a PCR target for the DNA-damage
induction, we have effectively increased DNA-
damage by up to threefold. This is partly explained
by the greater proportion of DNA damage induced
in the target region and also the cellular shielding
that can reduce DNA damage by up to 50% [29].
We have also con®rmed the effect of target size
on polymerase inhibition [8], in which longer tar-
gets are more sensitive to DNA-damage induction
and hence detection with the PI assay. However,
there appeared to be a trade-off between assay sen-
sitivity and robustness with regard to longer PCR
targets, with the longest target providing greater
sensitivity but being less reliable with regard to
ampli®cation, supporting previously published data
[8]. Given that variation in ampli®cation ef®ciency
in the PI assay may yield false positive results, we
employed the longest target which produced con-
sistent ampli®cation, in this case 1.7 kb.
The actual type of DNA damage induced by UV-C,
UV-B, MNU, ENU, and N-OH-PhIP was not identi®-
able with the PI assay. In the case of UV-C and UV-B,
there are two main possible culprits, pyrimidine
dimers and 6-4 photoproducts. Given that pyrimi-
dine dimers are induced at approximately threefold
the level of 6-4 photoproducts [30], these are
probably responsible for polymerase inhibition;
however, the contribution of 6-4 photoproducts
and indeed other UV-induced damage types (includ-
ing strand breaks) cannot be discounted. In the
cases of ENU and MNU, there are many types of
damage capable of blocking polymerases. These
include damage by N3-adenine, N7-guanine, O6-
alkylguanine, O4-alkylthymidine, and O2-alkylthy-
midine. MNU and ENU are known to induce differ-
ing patterns of alkyl damage [31]. For example, it is
known that MNU induces signi®cant levels of the
induces relatively more DNA damage at thymidine
bases [27]. This may possibly explain the greater
polymerase inhibition of MNU compared with ENU
in this study, if O6-alkylguanine is a polymerase-
inhibiting lesion. In the case of N-OH-PhIP, the
culprit DNA damage is thought to be due to
formation of a C8-guanine DNA lesion [23].
In the cases of the polymerase inhibition by ENU
and N-OH-PhIP, there appeared to be a threshold
effect. Both compounds failed to induce polymerase
inhibition at low doses and were only positive with
the PI assay at higher doses. In addition, the dose-
response curves of these compounds were linear as
opposed to the curves of the other genotoxins
tested. This mimics data obtained on the polymer-
ase inhibition of 2-acetylamino¯uorene [5], in
which it was found that DNA damage had to be
induced at more than 30 guanines before a poly-
merase inhibition effect was visible. This may cor-
respond to DNA damage that is readily bypassed by
the polymerase unless a critical number of bases are
induced. Furthermore, the fact that polymerase
inhibition appears in some cases not to be complete,
with a plateau effect at high doses, may also
represent polymerase-bypass events.
It should be mentioned that there may not
necessarily be a direct correlation between DNA-
damage formation, mutagenicity, and carcinogeni-
city. The DNA damage that is readily bypassed may
be highly mutagenic and hence potentially carcino-
genic, and vice versa. This is perhaps illustrated by
the results shown here with ENU and N-OH-PhIP.
ENU forms weak polymerase-arresting lesions, but
these are known to be both mutagenic and carcino-
genic, whereas N-OH-PhIP forms relatively more
polymerase-arresting lesions but is, in general, a
weaker mutagenic agent than ENU. This may
explain why ENU in this study showed polymerase
inhibition at doses twofold to ®vefold higher than
those used to induce mutations (5 mM instead of 1±
2 mM). This consideration should be borne in mind
when interpreting genotoxicity data obtained by
the PI assay. A positive result in the PI assay may not
indicate a potent mutagen, and a negative result
may not necessarily indicate an inert compound.
The genotoxicity data would have to be assessed in
the light of data from other genotoxicity (muta-
genicity) assays, which is the main reason why a
battery of complementary tests assessing different
The PI assay, in the form described here, is unable
to detect genotoxins requiring metabolic activation.
We performed several studies using both S9 mix
(crude extract of liver P450 enzymes) and micro-
enzymes (Gentest Corporation, Woburn, MA) in
order to activate indirectly acting DNA-damaging
agents. These studies were performed with the acti-
vation dependent DNA damaging agents benzo[a]-
pyrene, a¯atoxin B1, and 2-amino¯uorene (results
not shown). However, both the P450-containing
enzyme preparations employed were found to con-
tain contaminating nuclease activity that destroyed
the target sequence, preventing re-ampli®cation. It
is envisaged that the employment of puri®ed P450
enzymes (not currently available) should be capable
of allowing such indirectly acting DNA damaging
agents to be identi®ed with the PI assay.
Current work in our laboratory is centered on
employing the PI assay as a research tool in
Given that the DNA target currently employed in
the assay is the p53 gene, data on DNA damage
accumulation in this key gene may be relevant to
carcinogenesis (although important DNA damage
data are obtainable with any gene target). These
studies are centered on the detection of DNA
damage induced by two known carcinogens, solar
UV light and reactive oxygen species. In addition
to the detection of such damage, the protec-
sunscreens and the role of antioxidants in soaking
up free radicals is being investigated. Hence, the
PI assay can be employed both as a screen for
DNA-damaging agents in a medium- to high-
throughput format and also can provide infor-
mation on DNA-damage induction, repair, and
prevention in a key gene heavily implicated in
